entries.title: About the Course
entries.content:
Each year in High School, students read one of the gospels along with several other New Testament epistles. For 2026-2027, they read the gospel of Luke along with Galatians, Philippians, and Colossians. Using their study Bibles and other resources, students encounter scripture and seek to understand the cultural, historical, and theological context of their readings in order to more deeply appreciate the richness of the Bible and the humility needed in order to live out God's word today.

This topic is included in the following course(s): Bible: Grade 9

entries.title: Placement & Combining Tips
entries.content: Appropriate for any HS grades. Students new to reading the Bible for themselves or unused to commentaries or study notes may find the lessons in Grades 7-8 a gentler introduction to these study practices.

rows.label: New Testament: Grade 9
rows.books: NIV Cultural Backgrounds Study Bible: Bringing to Life the Ancient World of Scripture||Introducing the New Testament: A Historical, Literary, and Theological Survey||Luke for Everyone

entries.content:
Permission to print for non-commercial use. See Alveary group use policy to use lessons
in a group context.

LINKS: Click text or scan the QR code in the top corner of the lesson plan pages to view
online resources associated with the lessons.

Responsibility for previewing all links rests with the teacher. All links were checked at the
time of publication; however, websites change frequently and may contain objectionable
content. Please report broken links by contacting us through our website.

books.title: NIV Cultural Backgrounds Study Bible: Bringing to Life the Ancient World of Scripture
Introducing the New Testament: A Historical, Literary, and Theological Survey

blocks.text: Take a moment to recall what you read previously in the same book or topic.

Read the passage assigned or read for the number of minutes allocated. If a book is challenging for you, try stopping to narrate to yourself silently now and then to make sure you are tracking. Or you could listen to an audiobook version of the text and read along. You may also want to read aloud to yourself. If a book is an easy read, enjoy the ride! Keep an eye out for quotes to put in your Commonplace Book or events or characters to put down in your Book of Centuries.

Process and integrate your reading by narrating either orally or in writing. Some lesson plans will give you a suggestion of how best to narrate. You might want to set a timer to keep yourself on track, so you stop reading with enough time to narrate.

Read notes in your Study Bible or commentary. Look up places mentioned in the text in your Bible atlas. Add something to your Book of Centuries or Commonplace Book. Jot down additional reflections and thoughts in your Bible notebook or share with others.

Read over the lesson plans to familiarize yourself with the content for accountability and interest. If doing the lessons together, use a picture, a map, or an engaging question to rouse students' interest before reading. You may also highlight one or two unfamiliar terms if needed. If in a classroom context, you may choose to leave larger doctrinal conversations for students to have with their parents at home.

lessons.title: 30m New Testament: Grade 9 - Lesson 1
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Cultural Backgrounds Study Bible, Chronological Guide

➜ INTRO
Each year in HS, we will be reading one of the Gospels along with our study of the rest of the New Testament books. This year, we will be reading Mark along with Galatians, Philippians, and Colossians. It is helpful to be continually reminded of who Jesus is and how he interacted with people. The Gospel accounts were designed to be read many times. There will be insights gained that you would only pick up after multiple readings and by paying attention to repeated words and themes. 

We will make use of the Cultural Backgrounds Study Bible notes and maps, and some Bible Project videos/articles/podcasts to help us understand what Mark and Paul are saying to their audiences. Then we can think about what new thoughts about God we gain in the process. You do not need to read all the study notes for all the verses. Pick and choose based on which verses you have questions about. Use a digital or physical notebook to record your thoughts as you work your way through the readings. 

➜ VIEW & NARRATE
Today, we will set the stage for reading Mark by gaining some context. Narrate silently in your head or by talking to yourself to go over the main points. Or, jot them down in your journal.
∞ Video Link: How to Read the New Testament
∞ Video Link: How to Read the Gospel
∞ Video Link: Mark Summary
 
➜ PRINT
You may want to print out the summary of the book to reference as you read.
∞ Image Link: Mark

➜ MAPWORK
When places are named in the text, refer to a map in the study Bible. Finding the places on the map is very helpful for seeing the stories in the context of the land. Sketching your own maps of journeys or regions can be helpful too.
lessons.teacherNotes:
• NOTE 
This journal option contains only the Bible book text and space to write.

∞ Link: ESV Scripture Journal Mark

lessons.title: 30m New Testament: Grade 9 - Lesson 2
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Cultural Backgrounds Study Bible, Chronological Guide

➜ READ  
Mark 1

➜ NARRATE & RESPOND
Silently or orally tell back to yourself what you read. Then, in your journal, respond to what you read by putting down some of your thoughts or questions. Just like in nature study, when we ask questions such as What did I notice? What did I wonder? Or what did this remind me of? We can ask the same questions about what we read. 

➜ LISTEN & DISCUSS
∞ Podcast Link: Anointed
• Listen from 11:06-29:03 about the first verses of Mark. Jot down any thoughts afterwards.
lessons.teacherNotes:
★ NOTE
Instead of reading Mark for some of the lessons, consider watching and listening to Mark via the LUMO Project, which only uses the actual text of Mark but has people acting while it is read. There are many versions and many languages available. You might consider this resource for your modern language studies, too.

∞ Video Link: Lumo Project Mark

lessons.title: 30m New Testament: Grade 9 - Lesson 3
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Cultural Backgrounds Study Bible, Chronological Guide

➜ RECAP 
What did you read last time?

➜ READ
Mark 2-3

➜ NARRATE & RESPOND
Silently or orally tell back to yourself what you read. Then, in your journal, respond to what you read by putting down some of your thoughts or questions. Just like in nature study, when we ask questions such as What did I notice?, What did I wonder?, or What did this remind me of?, we can ask the same questions about what we read. Repeat this same process as you work through the lessons.
lessons.teacherNotes:
★ NOTE
The Bible Project has an app with a weekly playlist to go deeper into many topics for continued study.

∞ Info Link: Bible Project App
